summary:
Chaco Canyon (c. 850–1150 AD) was the ceremonial and political center of the Ancestral Pueblo (Anasazi) civilization of the American Southwest, located in the high desert of modern New Mexico, and representing one of the most ambitious architectural achievements of pre-Columbian North America north of Mexico. The canyon contained a network of 'great houses' — massive multi-story masonry apartment complexes — of which Pueblo Bonito (with 800 rooms and 37 kivas arranged in a D-shaped structure rising four to five stories) was the largest and most sophisticated prehistoric building in North America until skyscrapers in the 19th century.

Chaco Canyon functioned as a regional center for a network of communities spanning over 100,000 square kilometers, connected by an extraordinary system of roads — straight, engineered highways up to 9 meters wide, visible from aerial survey, radiating in cardinal directions from the canyon — whose purpose remains debated (trading networks, pilgrimage routes, or both). The great houses' architectural sophistication included precisely aligned astronomical features: certain windows at Pueblo Bonito are aligned to solstice and equinox sunrises, suggesting complex astronomical knowledge. Chaco was abandoned around 1150 AD, likely due to a combination of prolonged drought, resource depletion from over-extraction of timber (thousands of trees were carried from forests 80 kilometers away), and political or social disruption. The Chacoans appear to have dispersed northward to [[event:2312|Mesa Verde]] and other areas, carrying their architectural traditions and ceremonial knowledge with them.
